name: Vilches – tour around the Guadalén Dam and La Fernandina
name_original: Vilches – vuelta por las Presa de Guadalén y La Fernandina
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Vilches traverse a landscape at the foot of the Sierra Morena mountains, offering varied elevations and scenic climbs. The region features the Guadalén Reservoir, providing waterside sections. Cyclists encounter diverse natural settings, including extensive olive groves, oak groves, and pine forests. This varied terrain provides a range of road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Vilches The most popular road cycling route is Vilches – tour around the Guadalén Dam and La Fernandina, a 30.8 miles (49.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 17 minutes to complete. This route circles the Guadalén Dam and passes through La Fernandina. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Parque Natural de Despeñaperros — round trip through the Collado de los Jardines and Mirador Estacion de Santa Elena, a moderate 24.2 miles (39.0 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and views within the Parque Natural de Despeñaperros. Local road cyclists also love the Vadollano Roman Bridge – Plaza de Toros de Linares loop from Vilches, a 25.0 miles (40.3 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 46 minutes. Road cycling around Vilches is defined by mountainous terrain, extensive olive groves, and the Guadalén Reservoir. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate climbs to more challenging routes. The routes in Vilches are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.75 stars from over 9 reviews. More than 1700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Vilches's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find around Vilches?
A: Vilches offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, from moderate climbs to more challenging ascents, traversing varied landscapes. You'll find routes that circle the picturesque Guadalén Reservoir, wind through extensive olive groves, and explore oak and pine forests at the foot of the Sierra Morena mountains. There are over 90 routes available, catering to different ability levels.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Vilches?
A: Yes, Vilches has a few easier road cycling routes. While many routes involve moderate climbs, there are 2 routes specifically rated as easy. These typically feature less elevation gain and can be a good starting point for beginners or families looking for a more relaxed ride. For instance, routes around the Guadalén Reservoir might offer flatter sections.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling routes in the Vilches area?
A: For those seeking a challenge, Vilches offers 37 difficult road cycling routes. These routes often feature significant elevation gains and longer distances, particularly those venturing deeper into the Sierra Morena mountains. An example of a route with notable elevation is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1274417571/parque-natural-de-despenaperros-round-trip-through-the-collado-de-los-jardines-and-mirador-estacion-de-santa-elena" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Natural de Despeñaperros — round trip through the Collado de los Jardines and Mirador Estacion de Santa Elena&lt;/a&gt;, which includes substantial climbing within the natural park.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available around Vilches?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Vilches are circular, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996072528/vilches-tour-around-the-guadalen-dam-and-la-fernandina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vilches – tour around the Guadalén Dam and La Fernandina&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a scenic loop around the reservoir. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40315860" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vadollano Roman Bridge – Plaza de Toros de Linares loop from Vilches&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I expect to see along the road cycling routes?
A: The road cycling routes around Vilches offer stunning natural scenery. You'll encounter the picturesque Guadalén Reservoir, extensive olive groves, and diverse forests. Notable viewpoints include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5620563" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balcón de Despeñaperros Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5418434" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Desfiladero Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views of the mountainous terrain.

Q: Are there any waterfalls or unique natural monuments accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, you can incorporate visits to natural monuments into your road cycling adventures. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5289848" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Negrillo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; is a beautiful natural feature that can be reached, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5418382" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cimbarra Mill&lt;/a&gt; is another interesting natural monument in the vicinity.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Vilches?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Vilches, with an average score of 4.75 stars from over 9 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from the scenic waterside sections around the Guadalén Dam to the challenging climbs in the Sierra Morena foothills, and the ever-changing landscapes of olive groves, oak, and pine forests.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Vilches?
A: Given its location in Andalusia, Spain, at the foot of the Sierra Morena, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Vilches. Temperatures are milder, making longer rides more comfortable. Summers can be very hot, while winters are cooler but often still suitable for cycling, especially on sunny days.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural landmarks to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, beyond the natural beauty, you can discover historical and cultural landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4072358" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Empedraillo&lt;/a&gt; is a historical site, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of Linarejos&lt;/a&gt; offers a cultural point of interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40315860" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vadollano Roman Bridge – Plaza de Toros de Linares loop from Vilches&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, passes by the Roman Bridge.

Q: Are there any hidden gem road cycling routes in Vilches that are less frequented?
A: While many routes are popular, exploring some of the 54 moderate or 37 difficult routes that are not highlighted as the most popular can lead you to less frequented paths. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40016814" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Elena Fountain – La Aliseda loop from Santa Elena&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38167828" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Aliseda – Santa Elena Fountain loop from Santa Elena&lt;/a&gt; offer beautiful scenery and a chance to discover quieter sections of the region.

Q: What amenities, like cafes or recreation areas, are available along the routes?
A: While specific cafes on every route are not detailed, the region around Vilches, including nearby towns like Linares, offers amenities. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2638820" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Aliseda Recreation Area&lt;/a&gt; is a designated spot where you might find facilities. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer or more remote routes.
tips: Ruin of a stone bridge over the Guarrizas river from Roman times. Currently, a modern bridge crosses the A-312 road, providing a good viewpoint of the old bridge.
The road over the hydroelectric dam in the Fernandia reservoir. The reservoir that collects water from the Guarrizas and Despeñaperros rivers.

A small town located in the foothills of Sierra Morena. The castle of Cerro de la Virgen stands on a hill overlooking the town below. Only a few elements remain of the castle, a tower, a tunnel with access to the vaulted castle and a demolished tower. The origins of the castle of Vilches are uncertain. The hill of the castle was continuously populated since Iberian times.
